paper_title: Fourier analysis of the planktonic foraminifer Neogloboquadrina pachyderma (Ehrenberg) from the Pleistocene Santa Barbara Formation, California

methods.description: Collection of rock samples from the lower member of the Santa Barbara Formation at the Bathhouse Beach locality for subsequent analysis.

steps.description: Eleven rock samples were collected at intervals from the lower member of the Santa Barbara Formation (Fig. 1). The outcrop is located in a road cut along Cabrillo Boulevard beginning just south of the paved walkwav between Santa Barbara College and City Park.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect geological samples from a specified location and formation.
inputs: Location: Bathhouse Beach, Santa Barbara, California
Formation: Santa Barbara Formation, lower member

methods.description: Preparation of collected rock samples to isolate fossil specimens.

steps.description: In the laboratory, samples were washed over a 75 pm screen
steps.searchable_summary: Wash geological samples using a sieve to separate components.
outputs: Washed sample residue retained on screen

methods.method_name: Neogloboquadrina pachyderma Specimen Sorting and Phenotype Identification
methods.description: Examination of prepared samples to obtain Neogloboquadrina pachyderma specimens and classify them into warm or cool water phenotypes.

steps.description: Of 352 specimens obtained from the eleven samples (Table l), 204 represent the warm water phenotype (Pl. 1, figs. 1-3) and 148 represent the cool water phenotype (Pl. 1, figs. 4-8).
steps.searchable_summary: Examine dried sample residue to extract and count fossil specimens.

steps.description: The warm and cool phenotype specimens in each sample were separated microscopically resulting in twenty two subsamples (Fig. 3).
steps.searchable_summary: Classify specimens into distinct phenotypes based on morphological characteristics using microscopy and separate them by sample.

methods.description: Analysis of the shape of individual Neogloboquadrina pachyderma specimens using Fourier analysis.

steps.description: The specimens were digitized from the dorsal side using a digital Graphic CAT-100 optical digitizer and the EDGE Fortran programme (Fogerty, 1985).
steps.searchable_summary: Digitize the shape outline of specimens from a specific view using an optical digitizer and software.

steps.description: The resultant matrix was converted to a data set consisting of a value for each harmonic using the Fast Fourier Transform (Brigham, 1974 (Ehrenberg) specimens collected from each sample. harmonic amplitude represents the relative contribution to the empirical shape of a characteristic shape component.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply Fast Fourier Transform to shape data to obtain harmonic amplitudes.
outputs: Data set of harmonic amplitudes for each specimen

methods.description: Application of various statistical methods (Chi-square, Cluster Analysis, Factor Analysis, Discriminant Analysis) to analyze the harmonic amplitude data and phenotype classifications.

steps.description: In order to determine the statistically significant harmonics controlling the shape of this species, chi square values were calculated on the highest and lowest amplitude of the first ten harmonics respectively (Fig. 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Chi-square test on harmonic amplitudes to identify statistically significant harmonics.

steps.description: Using the IMSL (1982) computer programme OCLINK, a Q-mode cluster analysis was made to compare the similarity of warm and cool phenotype specimens from the eleven sample levels utilizing the amplitudes of the first 16 harmonics (ranging from 0.0000-0.0030).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply Q-mode cluster analysis to group specimens based on harmonic amplitudes.
inputs: Harmonic amplitudes (first 16 harmonics) for warm and cool phenotype specimens from 11 samples (22 subsamples)
outputs: Cluster analysis results (e.g., dendrogram, cluster assignments)

steps.description: A Q-mode factor analysis, using IMSL (1982) computer programme OFCOEF, was performed to determine the end members within the 22 subsamples ( Table 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply Q-mode factor analysis to identify underlying factors or end members in the data.

steps.description: A discriminate function that best separated two previously determined end-member groups was calculated by using IMSL (1982) computer programme ODNORM.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate a linear discriminant function to separate two predefined groups.

steps.description: The constants of the discriminant function for the two groups were then used to classify all specimens in either of the two groups using the computer programme DISCRIM.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply a calculated discriminant function to classify all specimens into one of two groups.
